subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Survey Methodology
Undergraduate degree in Sociology from a mid-sized university
Completed graduate coursework in statistics, research design, and data analysis
Participated in a capstone project focused on survey design and data visualization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Survey Researcher
Responsible for designing, piloting, and analyzing surveys for research projects
Role includes collaborating with project stakeholders to define research questions
Involved in both the creation of survey instruments and the interpretation of collected data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the social science research sector
Conducts survey research for academic and nonprofit organizations
Occasionally supports projects for commercial market research clients

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a small research consulting firm with approximately 25 employees
Office environment is collaborative and project-driven
Team frequently works cross-functionally with other researchers and analysts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Specializes in customized survey research services
Clients include universities, nonprofits, and policy think tanks
Projects focus on social attitudes, program evaluation, and public opinion

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Less than 1 year of experience as a Survey Researcher
Started current position immediately after completing graduate studies
First full-time professional role in the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Entry-level professional
Reports to a senior survey methodologist
Works alongside other recent graduates and junior researchers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Designs survey questionnaires based on project objectives
Conducts literature reviews and consults with project stakeholders
Coordinates and analyzes pilot tests to refine survey instruments
Performs data cleaning, analysis, and visualization of survey results
Prepares reports and presentations summarizing findings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 40% of time on survey design and question development
About 25% of time is dedicated to data cleaning and analysis
Roughly 20% of time spent on meetings and stakeholder communication
10% involves pilot testing and troubleshooting survey instruments
5% allocated to reporting and visualization tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in drafting survey questions and formats
Final survey design decisions require supervisor approval
Can independently choose tools and methods for data analysis within team guidelines
Expected to bring forward suggestions for process improvements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Building surveys using platforms like SurveyMonkey and Google Forms is intuitive
Communicating with stakeholders to clarify project goals comes naturally
Creating basic survey visualizations and summary tables is straightforward

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Identifying question wording that avoids bias while maximizing response quality is difficult
Interpreting ambiguous or contradictory survey responses requires careful judgment
Designing surveys that work equally well across diverse respondent groups is complex
Balancing survey length with data needs can be challenging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Transferring survey data from collection platform to spreadsheets is repetitive
Cleaning and formatting respondent data involves routine steps
Generating basic summary statistics and charts could be automated
Performing initial checks for incomplete or invalid responses is data-heavy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Brainstorming and refining research questions is highly engaging
Collaborating with colleagues on innovative survey ideas is enjoyable
Manual data entry or cleaning feels tedious and time-consuming
Repetitive formatting of survey results for reports is less satisfying

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Discovering design flaws only after survey launch is frustrating
Switching between multiple software platforms can disrupt workflow
Manual error-checking of data is time-intensive and prone to oversight
Delays in stakeholder feedback can slow down the survey development process

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Enjoyment is highest when learning new techniques or seeing results visualized clearly
Productivity increases for tasks that match current skill set, such as using survey platforms
Feeling challenged by complex design problems can be motivating, but too much ambiguity reduces productivity
Repetitive tasks lower enjoyment but can be completed quickly with adequate tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Open and enthusiastic about exploring new survey and analysis tools
Enjoys experimenting with productivity-enhancing software features
Views technology adoption as essential for staying current in the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to user-friendly platforms like Google Forms and SurveyMonkey
Requires some onboarding for more complex analytical tools
Comfortable troubleshooting basic issues independently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate personal time to online tutorials for new tools
Seeks out short, focused training sessions over lengthy workshops
Prefers hands-on learning and immediate application in projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by potential to save time and improve data quality
Occasional barrier: heavy project workload limits time for self-training
Willing to learn if tool is directly relevant to daily workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers frequently share tech tips that encourage faster adoption
Management supports exploring new tools, especially if they improve accuracy or efficiency
Positive team culture makes experimenting with new technologies less intimidating

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses SurveyMonkey and Google Forms for survey creation and deployment
Quadric is regularly used for more complex survey logic or branching
Spreadsheets (Google Sheets, Excel) are used for data cleaning and analysis
Data visualization is done with Google Data Studio or embedded survey tool features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Email and calendar apps (Gmail, Google Calendar) for scheduling and communication
Slack for team communication and quick troubleshooting
Word processing software (Google Docs, MS Word) for drafting reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with common survey platforms such as Qualtrics and SurveyMonkey
Has some exposure to R or Python for statistical analysis, mainly through academic training
Aware of integration options between survey platforms and spreadsheets

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally experiences formatting issues when exporting data from survey platforms to spreadsheets
Manual intervention is needed to merge data from multiple sources
Limited direct integration between visualization tools and survey data platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI concepts such as natural language processing and machine learning classification
Comfortable with terminology like 'large language models', 'training data', and 'prompt engineering'
Learned about AI applications in data analysis during graduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses OpenAI's ChatGPT for question wording suggestions and summarizing feedback
Employs Google Bard for brainstorming and quick research
Has experimented with automated data cleaning tools with AI features

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily to draft, revise, and optimize survey questions
Applies AI tools to synthesize stakeholder feedback into actionable changes
Occasionally uses AI for preliminary data analysis and identifying response trends

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated alternative question phrasings improved pilot survey response rates
Sometimes LLMs suggest question types that are not suitable for target respondents, requiring manual adjustment
AI output occasionally overlooks nuanced research goals, highlighting the need for human review

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training provided by the company, but informal peer learning is common
Peers regularly share prompt engineering tips and effective AI workflows
Self-guided experimentation with AI tools is encouraged by management

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI, especially during survey design
Finds it helpful when AI tools offer suggestions as each section is drafted
Uses independent AI interactions for quick brainstorming or troubleshooting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for drafting and refining content
Occasionally uses AI in an advisor role for exploring new question formats
Relies on human team members for final decision-making and validation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Very open to AI-driven workflows that increase efficiency or survey quality
Actively seeks out AI features that reduce manual, repetitive work
Wants to ensure that AI-driven changes are transparent and easily reversible if needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and iterative refinement
Uses command-based prompts for specific, well-defined tasks like data summarization
Finds a hybrid approach most productive, depending on the context

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Survey design often requires nuanced understanding of respondent demographics
Interpreting ambiguous results depends on knowledge of social science concepts
Adapting questions to sensitive topics involves tacit awareness of cultural norms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI-generated suggestions for initial drafts and brainstorming
Always manually reviews AI outputs before finalizing survey instruments
Reserves critical decisions—such as final question selection—for human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive drafting, but humans to shape final survey content
Believes data cleaning can be largely automated with human spot checks
Wants humans to interpret nuanced or unexpected survey responses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for data processing and visualization if results are auditable
Feels threatened if AI tools bypass human quality checks in survey design
Wants override options for any automated changes to survey content

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: AI enhances the importance of critical thinking and domain expertise
Routine technical skills (e.g., data cleaning) are less critical as automation improves
Strong communication and interpretation skills remain essential

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Prompt engineering and crafting effective AI queries is now a valuable skill
Ability to evaluate and validate AI outputs has become a new responsibility
Staying updated on the capabilities and limitations of AI tools is increasingly important

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Some concern that entry-level data cleaning or basic analysis tasks may be replaced by AI
Believes creative and interpretive aspects of survey research are less likely to be automated
Views AI as a tool to augment, rather than replace, the core responsibilities of the role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always manually reviews and edits AI-generated survey content
Relies on built-in audit trails within survey platforms to track AI-driven changes
Prefers systems that allow for easy rollback of automated modifications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team has become more open to experimenting with new AI tools in the past year
Company encourages responsible AI use and emphasizes human oversight
No formal AI policy yet, but informal best practices are emerging among peers

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will increase efficiency and reduce human error in survey research
Expects AI to broaden access to high-quality survey design for smaller organizations
Recognizes potential risks of bias and over-automation if not carefully managed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports using AI for routine tasks but stresses the need for transparency
Concerned about the potential for AI-generated biases in survey design
Believes ethical guidelines and human review are essential when applying AI to sensitive topics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from real-time AI feedback on survey clarity and design best practices
Desires AI tools that can simulate respondent behavior before survey launch
Wants integrated AI-driven error detection for survey logic and branching

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates spending more time on research interpretation and less on manual data handling
Expects the role to shift towards strategic decision-making and stakeholder engagement
Predicts that prompt engineering will become a core competency for survey researchers

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Recommends providing structured training on AI tools relevant to survey research
Advocates for developing clear guidelines on when and how to apply AI in projects
Suggests piloting new AI features with small teams before large-scale rollout
